title: Michael O'Hare (chef)

infobox.value: (born June 1981) is a British chef from [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough "Middlesbrough"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), [England](/wiki/England "England").[[1]](#cite_note-GL2-1) He was chef-patron at [The Man Behind The Curtain](/wiki/The_Man_Behind_the_Curtain_(restaurant) "The Man Behind the Curtain (restaurant)") in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), which was awarded a [Michelin star](/wiki/Michelin_star "Michelin star") in October 2015.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) He also ran The Rabbit in the Moon at the [National Football Museum](/wiki/National_Football_Museum "National Football Museum") in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British chef

| Michael O'Hare | |
| --- | --- |

| Spouse | Amanda Gilby |
| Children | 1 |
| **Culinary career** | |
| Cooking style | Fusion |
| Rating(s)   * [Michelin Stars](/wiki/Michelin_Star "Michelin Star") [1 Michelin star](/wiki/Michelin_star "Michelin star")   [AA Rosettes](/wiki/AA_Rosette "AA Rosette") StarStarStarStar | |
| Previous restaurant(s)   * [The Man Behind The Curtain](/wiki/The_Man_Behind_the_Curtain_(restaurant) "The Man Behind the Curtain (restaurant)") [1 Michelin star](/wiki/Michelin_star "Michelin star")     The Rabbit in the Moon     Psycho Sandbar | |

| Website | [The Man Behind The Curtain](http://www.themanbehindthecurtain.co.uk/) |

**Michael O'Hare** (born June 1981) is a British chef from [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough "Middlesbrough"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), [England](/wiki/England "England").[[1]](#cite_note-GL2-1) He was chef-patron at [The Man Behind The Curtain](/wiki/The_Man_Behind_the_Curtain_(restaurant) "The Man Behind the Curtain (restaurant)") in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), which was awarded a [Michelin star](/wiki/Michelin_star "Michelin star") in October 2015.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) He also ran The Rabbit in the Moon at the [National Football Museum](/wiki/National_Football_Museum "National Football Museum") in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester").

O'Hare began cooking whilst at university before becoming a professional chef, gaining experience at various restaurants including [John Burton-Race](/wiki/John_Burton-Race "John Burton-Race") at [The Landmark](/wiki/The_Landmark_London "The Landmark London") and [Noma](/wiki/Noma_(restaurant) "Noma (restaurant)").

## Early life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_O%27Hare_(chef)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and career")]

O'Hare was born in [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Eston "Eston") England. He describes [Redcar](/wiki/Redcar "Redcar"), North Yorkshire, as his home town.[[1]](#cite_note-GL2-1) From the age of 11 to 18 he studied classical and modern ballet.[[4]](#cite_note-TI1-4) He briefly studied [aerospace engineering](/wiki/Aerospace_engineering "Aerospace engineering") at [Kingston University](/wiki/Kingston_University "Kingston University"), London, deciding to leave the course after a few months.[[5]](#cite_note-BH1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-YFD1-6) In his early twenties, he worked in a call centre in Thornaby-on-Tees for energy company [nPower](/wiki/Npower_(United_Kingdom) "Npower (United Kingdom)").

It was during his time at university at the age of 19 that O'Hare began cooking: "I just liked going to the supermarket. I wasn't making sauces and all that. Just buying a bit of fish and grilling it."[[4]](#cite_note-TI1-4)

Instead of attending catering college, he decided to begin his cooking career learning within professional kitchens, with his first job at Judges in [Yarm](/wiki/Yarm "Yarm").[[7]](#cite_note-BQ1-7) Other restaurants where he worked include [Seaham Hall](/wiki/Seaham_Hall "Seaham Hall"), working for John Burton-Race at The Landmark and Noma before becoming head chef at The Blind Swine in [York](/wiki/York "York").[[5]](#cite_note-BH1-5)[[7]](#cite_note-BQ1-7)[[4]](#cite_note-TI1-4)[[6]](#cite_note-YFD1-6)

In 2014, O'Hare opened his restaurant with the backing of Stephen Baylis, The Man Behind The Curtain, in Leeds, where he was chef-patron.[[8]](#cite_note-TSC1-8)

He has gained a reputation for creative and often unusual dishes. These include chocolate pudding with potato foam, raw prawn tails with cooked prawn brains and a dessert with baked potato custard.[[4]](#cite_note-TI1-4)[[9]](#cite_note-GL1-9) In 2015, O'Hare took part in [BBC2's](/wiki/BBC_2 "BBC 2") *[The Great British Menu](/wiki/The_Great_British_Menu "The Great British Menu")* where he made a dish entitled 'Emancipation' – a fish dish inspired by fish and chips served on a [canvas](/wiki/Canvas "Canvas").[[10]](#cite_note-M1-10)[[11]](#cite_note-BBC01-11)

In 2016, O'Hare became Creative Director of GG Hospitality, a company created by ex-professional footballers [Ryan Giggs](/wiki/Ryan_Giggs "Ryan Giggs") and [Gary Neville](/wiki/Gary_Neville "Gary Neville").[[12]](#cite_note-BigHospitality-12) The company, along with O'Hare, opened a 'space-age Asian' restaurant called The Rabbit in the Moon at the National Football Museum in Manchester on 3 January 2017.[[13]](#cite_note-BigHospitality2-13)[[14]](#cite_note-thecaterer_23Dec2016-14) O'Hare was also due to set up a fine dining restaurant – The Man Who Fell To Earth, and a less formal restaurant called Are Friends Electric within a new Manchester hotel at the [Manchester Stock Exchange](/wiki/Manchester_Stock_Exchange "Manchester Stock Exchange") owned by GG Hospitality, but these did not open.[[7]](#cite_note-BQ1-7)[[15]](#cite_note-MEN_23Dec2016-15)

In October 2023, O'Hare closed The Man Behind the Curtain and then reopened it as Psycho Sandbar opened in early 2024.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) It then re-closed permanently in October 2024.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-BBC_Nov2024-19) The company operating the restaurant was liquidated to pay off the restaurant's £1 million debt owed to twelve creditors, including at least £519,000 owed to [HM Revenue and Customs](/wiki/HM_Revenue_and_Customs "HM Revenue and Customs") and £366,818 owed to Relentless, a managing firm run by Neville until his resignation as a co-director in September 2024.[[19]](#cite_note-BBC_Nov2024-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_O%27Hare_(chef)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Media")]

O'Hare competed in BBC2's *The Great British Menu* in 2015. In 2016, he appeared on BBC's *[Masterchef](/wiki/MasterChef_(UK_TV_series) "MasterChef (UK TV series)")*.[[9]](#cite_note-GL1-9) He also made guest appearances on BBC's *[Saturday Kitchen](/wiki/Saturday_Kitchen "Saturday Kitchen")*[[21]](#cite_note-21) and on *Yes Chef* in 2016 on [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One").[[22]](#cite_note-22) In 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2023, 2024 and in 2025 he returned to *The Great British Menu* as a judge.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_O%27Hare_(chef)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

O'Hare has one child.[[25]](#cite_note-25) O'Hare has become well known for his individual personal style. He became instantly recognisable with his 'rockstar hair' after his appearance on *The Great British Menu* and says “I know for a fact there could have been TV shows off the back of that haircut.”[[10]](#cite_note-M1-10)[[26]](#cite_note-Telegraph1-26) He is a qualified pilot, gaining his licence in [Daytona](/wiki/Daytona_Beach,_Florida "Daytona Beach, Florida") a few years after leaving university.[[5]](#cite_note-BH1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-YFD1-6)

## Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_O%27Hare_(chef)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Television")]

* *The Great British Menu* – 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2022, 2023, 2024 and 2025.
* *Saturday Kitchen* – 2015
* *Masterchef* – 2016
* *Yes Chef* – 2016
